verses.text: The date of this Defence of his Flight must be placed early enough to fall within the lifetime, or very close to the death (§1. n.

verses.text: 1), of Leontius of Antioch, and late enough to satisfy the references (§6) to the events at the end of May 357 (see notes there), and to the lapse of Hosius, the exact date of which again depends upon that of the Sirmian Council of 357, which, if held the presence of Constantius, must have fallen as late as August (Gwatk.

verses.text: Athanasius not only refers to the lapse of Hosius, but by the quotation he makes from Gal. ii.

verses.book: Defence of His Flight. (Apologia de Fuga.)
verses.text: How early, then, does the first-named condition compel us to place the ‘Defence?’

verses.text: Upon the news of the death of Leontius reaching Italy (Soz. iv.

verses.book: Defence of His Flight. (Apologia de Fuga.)
verses.text: There he got himself elected bishop, assembled a council (Acacius and other Homœans), and wrote a synodal letter, expelling from the Antiochene Church those who dissented.

verses.book: Defence of His Flight. (Apologia de Fuga.)
verses.text: Some of the latter repaired to Ancyra with a letter from the semi-Arian George of Laodicea; at Ancyra, Basil assembled a small council (before Easter, April 12, 358, see D.C.B. i.

verses.book: Defence of His Flight. (Apologia de Fuga.)
verses.text: 73), which wrote to the Emperor protesting against the proceedings of Eudoxius.

verses.book: Defence of His Flight. (Apologia de Fuga.)
verses.text: To gain room for these events, at the very least five months, and probably more, must be allowed to elapse between the death of Leontius and April 12, 358.

verses.book: Defence of His Flight. (Apologia de Fuga.)
verses.text: Leontius must therefore have died in the summer (Gwatk. p.

verses.book: Defence of His Flight. (Apologia de Fuga.)
verses.text: 153, note), or at the very latest in October, 357.

verses.book: Defence of His Flight. (Apologia de Fuga.)
verses.text: We cannot, therefore, place the Apology much after this date, for the reference to Hosius shews—in addition to many other indications—how quickly Athanasius in his hiding-place was informed of current events.

verses.book: Defence of His Flight. (Apologia de Fuga.)
verses.text: The Apology was drawn forth by the charge of cowardice circulated against him by the Arianising party, especially by the three bishops named in §1.

verses.book: Defence of His Flight. (Apologia de Fuga.)
verses.text: After a preamble upon the motives of his accusers (1, 2), he shews that his own case is but part of a general system (3–5) of expatriation directed against orthodox bishops.

verses.book: Defence of His Flight. (Apologia de Fuga.)
verses.text: He then refers to the circumstance of the attack upon himself, and dwells at length upon the tyranny of George (6, 7) and the banishment of Egyptian and Libyan bishops.

verses.book: Defence of His Flight. (Apologia de Fuga.)
verses.text: This brings him to the argument (8–22) which gives its name to the tract.

verses.book: Defence of His Flight. (Apologia de Fuga.)
verses.text: After pressing the point that if flight be evil, those who persecute are the responsible cause (8, 9), and hinting at the real motive of their mortification at his escape (10), he defends his flight by the example first (10, 11) of the Scripture Saints, secondly of the Lord Himself (12–15).

verses.book: Defence of His Flight. (Apologia de Fuga.)
verses.text: From the latter, he returns to the conduct of the Saints, who, unlike the Lord (16), were unaware of their appointed time, yet fled or not (17) as circumstances and the direction of the Spirit required them to do.

verses.book: Defence of His Flight. (Apologia de Fuga.)
verses.text: The Saints if they fled were not moved to do so by cowardice, else how could their flight so frequently have been the occasion of divine communications (18–20), and how could such good (21, 22) have resulted from it?

verses.book: Defence of His Flight. (Apologia de Fuga.)
verses.text: As a pendant to this vindication of flight on principle comes a short (23) but weighty rebuke of persecution as inherently devilish τὸ δὲ διώκειν διαβολικόν ἐστιν ἐπιχείρημα.

verses.book: Defence of His Flight. (Apologia de Fuga.)
verses.text: From principle, Athanasius now passes to fact.

verses.book: Defence of His Flight. (Apologia de Fuga.)
verses.text: He gives a graphic description (24) of the night attack on the Church of Theonas, and shews (25, 26) how fully his action on that occasion is covered by the examples of the ancient Saints of God.

verses.book: Defence of His Flight. (Apologia de Fuga.)
verses.text: He concludes (26, 27) with a somewhat exasperated denunciation of his opponents, and a prayer for the frustration of their intrigues.

verses.book: Defence of His Flight. (Apologia de Fuga.)
verses.text: The Apology is a locus classicus on the duty of Christians under persecution.

verses.book: Defence of His Flight. (Apologia de Fuga.)
verses.text: Athanasius was not the first great bishop who felt called upon to defend his conduct in retreating ‘until the tyranny be overpast’ (see Cyprian, Ep.

verses.book: Defence of His Flight. (Apologia de Fuga.)
verses.text: His principles are laid down with regard to the common welfare.

verses.book: Defence of His Flight. (Apologia de Fuga.)
verses.text: Rashness must be avoided, with its tendency to a reaction (17, end), and its presumption in forestalling the time appointed by Providence for our death.

verses.book: Defence of His Flight. (Apologia de Fuga.)
verses.text: But neither must that time be evaded.

verses.book: Defence of His Flight. (Apologia de Fuga.)
verses.text: When our end must come, we must face it quietly.

verses.book: Defence of His Flight. (Apologia de Fuga.)
verses.text: Accordingly (22) it is a duty to escape when we can, and to hide when sought for rather than to follow the exceptional (ib.) action of certain martyrs in courting death.

verses.book: Defence of His Flight. (Apologia de Fuga.)
verses.text: It is uncertain to whom the ‘Defence’ was addressed: it was perhaps a ‘memorandum’ to be circulated wherever opportunity offered.

verses.book: Defence of His Flight. (Apologia de Fuga.)
verses.text: The tract has always been justly admired for its lucidity, force, and dignity.

verses.book: Defence of His Flight. (Apologia de Fuga.)
verses.text: It is quoted largely by Socrates (ii.
